Spatial errors and long-term persistence – examining VV 2012

Joachim Voth

Why worry about spatial errors?

Many empiricial papers in applied economics use cross-sectional data. If you run it through normal regression analysis, the estimator assumes that each observation is independent from the next one. This is not necessarily a good idea. Imagine you try to analyse temperatures using cross-sectional data from Germany. Obviously, Bonn and Cologne will have fairly similar temperatures – they are close to each other. So once you have data for Cologne, adding Bonn does not offer a lot of extra insight. Bonn and Munich are far from each other – these two observations carry a lot of information. This is clearly a function of distance – using every suburb of Bonn would add almost nothing in terms of extra variation. But of course it also depends on the variable: population is not highly spatially correlated; Bonn is tiny compared to Cologne, and the suburbs of Bonna are tiny compared to Bonn itself. If you estimate with variables that are “spatially correlated”, you tend to understate standard errors – the estimator “thinks” it has extra information when it doesn’t, and you end up overclaiming statistical significance.

The problem of spatial errors has been well-known for a long time. There are different ways to address it, none of them perfect. One thing people routinely do is to add spatial-unit fixed effects. Use a Rhineland dummy, and remove the fact that it’s warmer on average – Bonn and Cologne now enter with their differnece to the Rhineland average, and the aggregate no longer drives the results. You can also correct for the upward bias of the significance, inflating your standard errors to make sure they reflect how little information is in every geographical unit. That is an inexact science, and people have used Conley standard errors for a long time. There, u need to specify over what distance you think the correlated errors die out – where, between Bonn and Munich, you decide not to worry any more. That was the state of play about 10 years ago.

Now, recently, two scholars have entered the intellectual playpen of spatial error correction – Müller and Watson. For those who don’t know, this is like Haaland and Messi playing for the same team. They wrote and are writing a bunch of papers on how to do this right, and when they came out, Sascha Becker and I decided to get into their methods and try to see how they change results in spatial studies. We coded up their routines and made them available for anyone to play with – in R, Python and Stata – and described them in a recent Stata Journal article. This is no intellectual contribution – just a service to others who might find it useful. MW do two things. First, they bring some intellectual order: They distinguish between strong and weak spatial dependence. With weak dependence, your errors might be a bit off – but the main intellectual exercise, regressing variable A on B, is basically sound. With strong dependence, this is not the case – the spatial pattern is the message, as both A and B are driven by the same, underlying pattern.

Imagine regressing the prevalence of a particular bird species on the share of homes heated with oil across 500 counties. There is no sensible reason why oil heating should affect the bird population. But suppose the bird becomes less common as one moves from east to west because of changes in habitat, while oil heating also becomes less common from east to west because of settlement history and energy infrastructure. A county-level regression could then produce a large coefficient, a high (R^2), and an enormous (t)-statistic.

The problem is not merely that neighboring counties have correlated errors. The regression is effectively treating hundreds of counties as independent evidence for a relationship when, in reality, much of the result comes from one broad east–west movement. Had the two spatial patterns happened to slope in opposite directions, the estimated relationship would have had the opposite sign. The coefficient is therefore describing an accidental alignment of persistent spatial fields, not a stable relationship between oil heating and birds.

That is Müller and Watson’s central distinction. Under weak dependence, spatial correlation mainly distorts our assessment of uncertainty. Under strong dependence, it can generate the estimated relationship itself. In that case, correcting the standard errors alone is not enough: the coefficient and the apparent fit of the regression may both be spurious.

The MW solution to the uncertainty issue is SCPC - correcting standard errors. They already made a great stata routine available for that. Then, the heavy lifting for strong dependence: You first need to find out how bad it is. That is what their test battery for spatial dependence does. Then, the fix: Difference your variable. With time series, that was easy: Take the difference the value at time t and t-1. It’s slightly more complicated with spatial errors, but the intuition is the same. Now, after differencing, the overall trend is no longer driving results: It is small-scale variation. To stick with our example: Get rid of the East-West gradient in heating and bird presence, and see if it is present after the broad sweep has been taken out. If so, congrats, there is a finding; if not, too bad.

Persecution Perpetuated

Some 15 years ago, I published a 2012 QJE paper (with Nico Voigtländer) on how places that murdered their Jews during the wave of pogroms during the Black Death were also more anti-Semitic during the interwar period. It’s using spatial data, so it’s a natural question to ask, how much of this applies here?

First things first: We didn’t forget about spatial errors. In our main results table, we already showed - on par with the OLS - the results from ‘spatial matching’ – basically form pairs of cities, one with a medieval “treatment” (murdering their Jews), some without it. These pairs are, on average, 20km apart. Then you just compare the difference in 20C outcomes, for these two units – an ultra-hard form of spatial differencing, built right into the estimation setup. The result? The difference is tiny, and often favors the main finding – a huge persistence of persecution, with the same places attacking Jews and engaging in anti-Semitic acts.

So far, so good — but that was 2012. Let’s use the new toolkit.

The matching was then; Müller and Watson is now. They give us sharper instruments, and the right thing is to run them and see what happens. Their recipe has two steps, and I’ll take them in order: first, how bad is the spatial dependence here, really? Then, what happens when we actually scrub it out?

Step one: how bad is it?

You might assume a Black-Death pogrom — something that rolled across Germany in a wave in 1348–50 — would sit on the map as one big smooth blob. That is the dangerous case, the bird-and-oil-heating case: if the treatment is one broad spatial trend and the outcome is another, the regression is just lining up two gradients and calling it a finding.

It isn’t like that. Look at the map. The towns that murdered their Jews and the ones that didn’t are shuffled together — no east–west sweep, no north–south belt. The ninety “no-pogrom” towns turn up everywhere, including the crowded south. Put a number on it. Moran’s I measures how much neighbouring towns resemble each other; the medieval pogrom scores a feeble 0.12.

The share of Protestants, by contrast, scores 0.58 and colours the map in obvious regional bands. That is what a genuinely clustered variable looks like.

The correlogram makes it plain. It shows how strongly two towns move together as you pull them further apart. The vote shares, the anti-Semitism index and the Protestant share all start high and fade only slowly with distance — the fingerprint of a strong spatial trend. The medieval pogrom is flat as a pancake at every distance. It behaves almost like noise.

That is reassuring before we have corrected anything at all. The outcomes are strongly spatial; the treatment is not. The spurious-regression trap needs both blades of the scissors, and here we only have one.

Table 1. How clustered is each variable across the map?

Variable Moran’s I Low-frequency share In plain words
Medieval pogrom, 1349 — the treatment +0.12 16% barely clustered — almost noise
% Protestant, 1925 — control +0.58 50% strongly clustered
% Jewish, 1925 — control +0.21 26% mildly clustered
ln population, 1928 — control +0.10 14% barely clustered
1920s pogrom — outcome −0.01 5% essentially random
DVFP vote, 1924 — outcome +0.62 58% strongly clustered
NSDAP vote, 1928 — outcome +0.38 33% clustered
Anti-Semitism index — outcome +0.55 50% strongly clustered

Moran’s I: how much neighbouring towns resemble each other (0 = none). Low-frequency share: how much of the variable is a broad, map-wide trend rather than local wiggle. The treatment sits at the bottom with log population; the strong spatial structure is all in the outcomes and the Protestant share.

Step two: correct it anyway.

Diagnosis is cheap; let’s do the surgery. First, SCPC — the Müller–Watson standard-error fix (the regression and panel version) — which stops us kidding ourselves about how much independent information the map really holds. Second, the heavy artillery: spatial differencing. Strip the broad trend out of every variable and see whether the medieval-to-modern link is still standing in what is left.

Read the results. The short version: most of it holds.

The link from medieval pogroms to 1920s pogroms — the purest “they did it again” — survives everything, and if anything gets stronger. The 1928 Nazi vote and our summary index of interwar anti-Semitism both stay significant once space is handled properly. The one that folds is synagogue attacks in 1938 — Kristallnacht. It is significant in every gentler test, but once we tear out the whole spatial trend, it is gone (p = 0.40). The PCA that extracts common patterns in the outcome variables holds up remarkably well.

Table 2. Does the effect survive proper spatial correction?

Outcome Effect p, as published p, spatial-robust Survives at 10%?
1920s pogrom +0.061 0.008 0.008 yes ✓
NSDAP vote, 1928 +0.014 0.013 0.058 yes ✓
Anti-Semitism index +0.29 0.029 0.052 yes ✓
Synagogue attacks, 1938 +0.124 0.018 0.400 no ✗

“Spatial-robust” is the Müller–Watson correction (SCPC, plus spatial differencing where the diagnostics call for it).

Notice that the local matching — those 20 km pairs — kept the synagogue result alive, while global trend-removal kills it. The two methods disagree because they ask different questions. Matching asks: did nearby treated and untreated towns differ? Differencing asks: is anything left once the big regional pattern is gone? For synagogue burnings, the answer to the second question is no.

After differencing, are we left with clean, trend-free leftovers, or is there still a spatial pattern lurking in the residuals? We tested. Every outcome comes back stationary — the differencing did its job. Whatever remains is weak, local wiggle, precisely the sort SCPC is built to mop up.

Table 3. After differencing, is the broad trend really gone?

Outcome Test for a leftover trend (p) Residuals
1920s pogrom 0.04 stationary ✓
NSDAP vote, 1928 0.003 stationary ✓
Anti-Semitism index 0.01 stationary ✓
Synagogue attacks, 1938 0.02 stationary ✓

A low p means we reject a leftover map-wide trend in the residuals — the differencing worked, and only weak local variation is left (the kind the corrected standard errors handle).

Bottom line

The headline — that the same towns which slaughtered their Jews in 1349 were still more anti-Semitic 600 years later — does not rest on a spatial mirage. The treatment isn’t a smooth trend to begin with; the pogrom-to-pogrom persistence is rock solid; the voting result survives proper spatial-robust inference. One outcome, the synagogue burnings of 1938, is fragile to the most aggressive correction. What about the overall picture? Look at the index of overall anti-Semitic activity, created across multiple indicators to identify and measure common patterns. It survives even the most aggressive combination of corrections – differencing and SCPC error correction. You can argue with each individual indicator; spatial errors make no difference, except for one variable; and the overall picture does not change.

References

  • Voigtländer, N., and H.-J. Voth (2012). “Persecution Perpetuated: The Medieval Origins of Anti-Semitic Violence in Nazi Germany.” Quarterly Journal of Economics 127(3): 1339–1392.
  • Müller, U. K., and M. W. Watson (2022). “Spatial Correlation Robust Inference.” Econometrica 90(6): 2901–2935.
  • Müller, U. K., and M. W. Watson (2023). “Spatial Correlation Robust Inference in Linear Regression and Panel Models.” Journal of Business & Economic Statistics 41(4): 1050–1064.
  • Müller, U. K., and M. W. Watson (2024). “Spatial Unit Roots and Spurious Regression.” Econometrica 92(5): 1661–1695.
  • Becker, S. O., P. D. Boll, and H.-J. Voth (2026). “Testing and Correcting for Spatial Unit Roots in Regression Analysis.” Stata Journal 26(2): 177–202. Code and worked examples: spatial-spur.github.io.
